coredata.dc:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets coupled with carbon dots and BiOI nanoparticles: Boosting visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Seifzadeh, Davod
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have developed a novel approach for depositing carbon dots (CDs) and BiOI nanoparticles onto the surface of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets (gCN-NS). The synthesized materials were subsequently characterized by means of XRD, EDX, SEM, HRTEM, UV–vis DRS, FT-IR, TGA, XPS, PL, and BET instruments. The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanocomposites was evaluated by degradations of Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), and Fuchsine under visible-light illumination. The obtained results revealed that the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites exhibited much better photocatalytic performance compared to the bare and binary photocatalysts due to the enhanced visible light absorption and improved charge separation. The gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite displayed superior photocatalytic performance for the removal of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine, which is about 29.5, 17.7, 10.9, and 16.5 times higher than that of the gCN. A good recyclability was also observed for the ternary nanocomposite after five cycles. Based on the obtained results, possible mechanism for the substantial enhancement of the photocatalytic performance via depositing the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles onto the surface of gCN-NS was discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanosheets
Carbon dots
BiOI nanoparticles
Photocatalytic performance
Organic pollutants

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCNNS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI.
(a) EDX spectra for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples. (b–g) EDX element mapping images for the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM and (b) HRTEM images of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite: wide scan spectrum (a) and high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Bi 4f, and (f) I 3d.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples. (b) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI. (c) Digital photographs of (A) gCN, (B) gCN-NS, (C) gCN-NS/CDs, (D) gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), (E) gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (10%), (F) gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (20%), (G) gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%), and (H) gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (40%) powders. (d) Plots of (αhv)2 versus hv for the samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TG curves of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI.
Photodegradation of RhB over the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI.
UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation over the (a) gCN-NS, (b) gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), (c) gCN-NS/CDs, and (d) gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different compositions. (b) PL spectra for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposites. (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption data for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples.
Possible mechanism for the degradation of organic pollutants over the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites.
Photodegradation rate constants of RhB over the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite in presence of different scavengers.

(a) Photodegradation rate constants of RhB over the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite calcined at different temperatures. (b) and (c) SEM images, and (d) PL spectra of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite before and after calcination at 400 °C for 2 h.
Recyclability of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite for the photodegradation of RhB.
Photodegradation rate constants of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine over the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) under visible-light irradiation.
Schematic illustration for preparation of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites.
Weight percentages of elements present in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite.
The textural properties of gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets coupled with carbon dots and BiOI nanoparticles: Boosting visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have developed a novel approach for depositing carbon dots (CDs) and BiOI nanoparticles onto the surface of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets (gCN-NS). The synthesized materials were subsequently characterized by means of XRD, EDX, SEM, HRTEM, UV–vis DRS, FT-IR, TGA, XPS, PL, and BET instruments. The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanocomposites was evaluated by degradations of Rhodamine B (RhB), methylene blue (MB), methyl orange (MO), and Fuchsine under visible-light illumination. The obtained results revealed that the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites exhibited much better photocatalytic performance compared to the bare and binary photocatalysts due to the enhanced visible light absorption and improved charge separation. The gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite displayed superior photocatalytic performance for the removal of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine, which is about 29.5, 17.7, 10.9, and 16.5 times higher than that of the gCN. A good recyclability was also observed for the ternary nanocomposite after five cycles. Based on the obtained results, possible mechanism for the substantial enhancement of the photocatalytic performance via depositing the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles onto the surface of gCN-NS was discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, serious environmental issues related to water contamination with various synthetic chemicals are alarming due to rapid industrialization and an increase in human activity [1–3]. Hence, providing fresh water is vital for all ecosystems. On the other hand, the reduction of carbon dioxide to fuels and the production of hydrogen fuel are promising strategies for providing renewable and green energy [4,5]. Therefore, many substantial efforts have been made to provide low-cost and efficient strategies to address environmental and energy crises [6–8]. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis is known as a promising technology to solve these issues in the future [9–12]. Heterogeneous photocatalytic processes can utilize solar energy to find solution to these crises [13–18]. Since the pioneering work of Wang and coworkers [19], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, abbreviated as gCN) with a two-dimensional polymeric structure has attracted much attention for various applications [19]. Owing to its fascinating characteristics, including easy synthesis, low-cost starting compounds, and considerable physicochemical stability, gCN has shown high potential to be one of the most feasible next generation photocatalysts [20,21]. It has been demonstrated that gCN with the band gap of 2.7 eV can be used as a visible-light-active photocatalyst, particularly after some modifications to decrease the recombination rate of the photoinduced electron–hole (e‒/h+) pairs and enhance the visible-light absorption [22,23]. The generally applied modification strategies include morphology control, anionic and cationic doping, integrating with other semiconductor having matched energy levels, and nanostructure construction [24–35].

Here, we have applied a combined strategy of morphology control, nonmetallic doping, and integration with a narrow-bandgap semiconductor to improve the photocatalytic performance of gCN. In this context, the gCN nanosheets (gCN-NS) were first fabricated by a thermal method [36]. For enhancing the visible-light absorption and fast transfer of the photoinduced e‒/h+ pairs, carbon dots (CDs) were deposited over the gCN-NS. Finally, BiOI nanoparticles were combined with the formed gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposites to fabricate ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites. Thanks to its narrow band gap (1.77–1.92 eV) [37], BiOI was introduced into the gCN-NS/CDs system to enhance the visible-light harvesting ability of gCN-NS, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The prepared ternary nanocomposites were systematically characterized and their photocatalytic performance was evaluated toward the photodegradation of organic pollutants under visible light. The results demonstrated that the BiOI content, calcination temperature, and scavengers of reactive species play an important role in the photodegradation of organic pollutants under visible-light irradiation. Possible mechanism for the substantial enhancement of the photocatalytic performance is also discussed.

Synthesis of gCN and gCN-NS: The gCN powder was synthesized by heating melamine (Loba Chemie, 99.2%) according to the procedure reported elsewhere [38]. In the synthesis of gCN-NS, 2 g of bulk gCN was powdered in a mortar and placed in a petri dish. Afterwards, the powder was calcined in air at 520 °C for 2 h [39,40].

Synthesis of CDs: First, 3 g of citric acid (Merck, 99%) and 3 g of urea (Merck, 99%) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to obtain a transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a microwave oven (750 W) and irradiated for 5 min. In this process, the product obtained a dark-brown color. The resultant product was maintained at 60 °C for 1 h in an oven to remove the volatile molecules and later dissolved in 180 mL of deionized water. The CDs were separated from the agglomerated particles by high-speed centrifugation (7500 rpm) for 10 min [41].

Synthesis of gCN-NS/CDs: Briefly, 0.4 g of bulk gCN and 0.5 mL of CDs-containing suspension were mixed in 100 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication (Bandelin ultrasonic, 9HD 3100, 20 kHz, 75 W) for 5 min and left for drying in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h. To prepare gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite, the dried sample (1 g) was placed in an open alumina crucible and subjected to thermal exfoliation by heating at 520 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C/min [39,40]. The color of the resultant sample was light yellow.

Preparation of gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite: As shown in Scheme 1, gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite was synthesized by a precipitation route. For preparing the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite, in which the mass ratio of BiOI to gCN-NS/CDs is 30 wt%, Bi(NO3)3•5H2O (Merck, 99%) and NaI (Loba Chemie, 99.7%) were used as precursors of BiOI. First, 0.45 g of gCN-NS/CDs was dispersed in 150 mL of deionized water by ultrasonication for 5 min. Then, 0.207 g of Bi(NO3)3•5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and introduced into the suspension containing gCN-NS/CDs. The mixture was continuously stirred under magnetic stirring for 2 h. Subsequently, 0.064 g of NaI was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and added dropwise into the mixture. After mixing for another 1 h, the resulting orange precipitate was collected and washed thrice with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 24 h. The gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with 10, 20, 30, and 40 wt% BiOI were prepared under the identical synthesis conditions. For comparison, the gCN-NS/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite was also prepared under the identical conditions without CDs.

The XRD patterns were recorded by a Philips Xpert X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The morphology and nanostructures of the prepared samples were observed by SEM (1340VP, LEO) by applying an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and HRTEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL) by applying an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, respectively. The surface chemical composition and states of elements were analyzed by XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). The TGA was performed on a Linseis STA PT 1000 apparatus in air at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in the temperature range of 25–700 °C. The UV–vis DRS were measured using a Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer. The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a BRUKER VERTEX70 spectrometer. The PL spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength at 450 nm. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BET specific surface areas were measured by BELSORP-mini II. The UV–vis spectra of aliquots were measured using a Cecile 9000 spectrophotometer.

All the photocatalytic activity tests were performed in a cylindrical glass reactor with capacity of 400 mL containing a water circulation system to maintain the temperature at 25 °C. A 50 W LED lamp was positioned on the top of the photoreactor. In the typical photocatalysis reaction, 250 mL of aqueous solution containing RhB, MB, MO, and Fuchsine and 0.1 g of the photocatalyst sample were put in the reactor with an air bubbling system and mechanical stirrer to keep the suspension homogeneous. The suspension was kept in the dark for 60 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium and then the lamp was switched on to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. The suspension was sampled out at given time intervals and centrifuged to remove photocatalyst particles. The remaining concentrations were analyzed by measuring the absorption spectra of RhB (2.5 × 10–5 M), MB (2.5 × 10–5 M), MO (2.5 × 10–5 M), and fuchsine (9.20 × 10–6 M) at 553 nm, 664 nm, 477 nm, and 540 nm, respectively.

The phase structures of the prepared samples containing gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI were analyzed by X-ray diffraction and the XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 1. For the gCN and gCN-NS samples, the strong diffraction peak is observed at 27.4°, which is indexed to the (002) plane of gCN. Additionally, the weak diffraction peak at 13.1° is indexed to the (100) plane of gCN (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [42]. After coupling the gCN-NS with CDs, no further distinctive diffraction peaks of CDs can be observed due to the low content of CDs deposited over the gCN-NS [43]. For the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites, besides the characteristic diffraction peaks of gCN-NS, the sharp and intense diffraction peaks can be indexed to tetragonal BiOI (JCPDS No. 10-0445) [44]. The intensive diffraction peaks observed at 29.7°, 31.7°, 45.8°, and 55.3° are assigned to the (102), (110), (200), and (212) planes of BiOI, respectively. Other diffraction peaks belonging to BiOI have lower intensity [44]. Hence, the XRD results confirm the formation of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite by the proposed facile method.

The EDX spectra and EDX element mapping images are shown in Fig. 2. Particularly, the gCN and gCN-NS samples clearly indicate the characteristic peaks of C and N elements. Due to the presence of different functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl in CDs, the EDX spectrum of gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite shows peaks corresponding to C, N, and O elements [45,46]. As expected for the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite, the separate peaks for C, N, O, Bi, and I elements are noticed. The weight percentages of elements present in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite are listed in Table 1. Similar to the XRD results, the EDX data also demonstrates the presence of gCN, CDs, and BiOI in the prepared ternary nanocomposite. Moreover, the EDX element mapping images shown in Fig. 2b–g confirm the homogenous distribution of elements in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite.

The morphology and nanostructures of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite were characterized by SEM and HRTEM, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. In the SEM image (Fig. 3a), the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles can be seen on the surface of sheet-like gCN. In Fig. 3b, the HRTEM image confirms that the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles are in intimate contact with gCN nanosheets. The HRTEM image also presents the interplanar spacings of 0.325 nm [47], 0.320 nm [45], and 0.304 nm [48] corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of gCN-NS, the (002) plane of CDs, and the (102) plane of BiOI, respectively.

The surface chemical composition and states of elements present in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalyst were studied by XPS. In Fig. 4a, the wide XPS spectrum of the ternary nanocomposite shows peaks belonging to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, and I 3d. No characteristic peaks for other elements were detected, suggesting the purity of the prepared nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 4b, the XPS peaks at binding energies in the range of 282–291 eV can be deconvoluted into four different peaks centered at 284.5 eV, 287.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 290.5 eV, corresponding to sp2 (CC), sp2 (NCN) of the broad asymmetric C 1s feature of gCN-NS, CO and OCO of CDs particles, respectively [49,50]. The O 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 4c shows four distinct peaks at binding energies of 530.5 eV, 531.3 eV, 532.5 eV, and 534.1 eV, which correspond to the lattice oxygen of BiOI, C–OH/C–O–C, C=O, and H–O–H, respectively [49,51]. The N 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 4d shows three peaks at binding energies of 398.8 eV, 400.18 eV, and 404.67 eV, which can be attributed to CNC, N(C)3, and amino groups as C–NH x , respectively [52]. In the Bi 4f XPS spectrum, two individual symmetric peaks appearing at 159.3 eV and 164.7 eV with a separation energy of 5.3 eV are assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 4e), which is characteristic of Bi3+ from BiOI [48]. In the I 3d XPS spectrum, the two peaks at 618.8 eV and 630.3 eV are attributed to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 (Fig. 4f), indicating that the main chemical state of I is −1 [48].

The FT-IR analysis was performed to determine the existing functional groups in the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples. In Fig. 5a, for all the samples, the broad absorption peaks in the range of 3000–3400 cm–1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H groups, which were associated with uncondensed amino groups and surface adsorbed H2O molecules, respectively [52]. The peaks observed between 1240 and 1650 cm–1 were related to the stretching modes of C–N and C=N heterocycle in CN with peaks centered at 1635 cm−1, 1563 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, 1322 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1, together with the peak at 809 cm−1 assignable to the stretching vibration of heptazine units [37,53]. For the gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite, the peaks in the range of 1600–1770 cm−1 and at 1431 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO and the bending vibration of –CH2, respectively [41,54]. The characteristic peak of BiOI was verified by the band at 508 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the Bi–O bond [37].

Optical properties of the prepared samples were characterized by UV–vis DRS. Fig. 5b shows the spectra of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with different contents of BiOI. Obviously, all the samples showed absorption edges in the visible-light region. The absorption edge of bulk gCN was found to be at 490 nm (E g = 2.7 eV). Compared with the gCN, the absorption edge of gCN-NS demonstrated a slight blue-shift (λ = 456 nm, E g = 2.8 eV), indicating the characteristic of the exfoliated gCN-NS [55]. After the modification of gCN-NS with CDs, the absorption edge shifted toward higher wavelengths. The absorption edge of binary gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite was located at 603 nm, whereas the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites can absorb visible light with absorption edge up to 630 nm, depending on the BiOI content. The enhanced response of the ternary nanocomposites for visible light can boost the photocatalytic performance, due to the generation of more photoinduced charge carriers under visible-light illumination. The increased harvesting of visible light with an increase in the BiOI content was also observed in the color change of the nanocomposite powders (Fig. 5c), turning from light yellow to dark orange.

The band gap energy (Eg ) of the resultant photocatalysts was estimated using the following equation:
(1)
α h υ
=
β
(
h υ −
E g
)
n / 2
in which α, h, υ, β, and Eg are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy, respectively. In this equation, the value of n depends on the characteristics of the transition in the material [56]. In this method, the Eg values were calculated by extrapolation of the linear parts of the curves obtained by plotting (αhυ) 2 versus hv and the results for the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites with
different contents of BiOI are displayed in Fig. 5d. The band gap of binary gCN-NS/CDs nanocomposite with absorption edge of 603 nm is 2.6 eV and that for the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite with absorption edge of 630 nm is 2.1 eV. In the case of gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite, there is a considerable decrease in the band gap relative to the pristine gCN, due to formation of heterojunctions between gCN, CDs, and BiOI.

Fig. 6 shows the TG curves of the synthesized samples to establish quantitative profiles and to display thermal stabilities. As shown, all the samples showed two-step mass losses. The first weight loss was observed at about 140 °C, due to desorption of the adsorbed water molecules. The next weight loss was observed at temperatures exceeding 400 °C, because of the burn-off of organic parts of the prepared samples. For the pure gCN, a rapid and obvious weight loss was observed at the temperature range of 490–650 °C, which was stemmed from almost a complete decomposition of gCN. The thermostability of gCN was decreased after converting gCN into gCN-NS and integrating it with CDs and BiOI. For the gCN-NS, binary, and ternary photocatalysts, the improved surface area through exfoliation and the loaded CDs and BiOI particles could act as active sites and adsorb oxygen from the air, leading to oxidizing gCN part of the photocatalysts at lower temperatures than the bulk gCN [57]. Furthermore, the organic parts of the prepared samples were calculated from the weight combustion after heating the samples over 700 °C to be 66.4, 82.9, 76.1, 62.3, and 56.4 wt% for the gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (10%), gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (20%), gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (40%) nanocomposites, respectively, which are roughly consistent with the BiOI content.

Photocatalytic activities of the prepared photocatalysts with same concentration were evaluated by degradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7, RhB has a substantial stability under the light irradiation. In addition, the photocatalytic performance of the gCN is not high due to the poor harvesting of visible light and high recombination rate of e‒/h+ pairs [42]. However, after exfoliation, the gCN-NS sample exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity compared with the gCN sample, due to decrease in the recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers and increased specific surface area [58]. Interestingly, the photocatalytic performance was drastically improved when the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles were simultaneously deposited over the gCN-NS. With increasing the BiOI content, the ternary nanocomposites showed an improved photocatalytic activity, and the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance among the samples, by completely degrading molecules of RhB within 120 min. In order to explore the adsorption ability of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite towards RhB, the adsorption process in dark condition was done. As shown in Fig. 7, it is evident that in the dark condition, adsorption process of RhB molecules over the ternary photocatalyst was reached to equilibrium after stirring the suspension for 60 min. Afterwards, concentration of RhB did not change and became constant. Therefore, the decrease of RhB under the light irradiation is solely related to the degradation process rather than adsorption.

Fig. 8 shows the typical UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB in aqueous solutions after the photodegradation reaction performed with the gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts in the range of 400–700 nm. Within 120 min of the photocatalytic reaction, about 36%, 44.7%, 76.7%, and 100% of RhB were degraded over the gCN-NS, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), gCN-NS/CDs, and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts, respectively. It is worth to mention that during photodegradation reactions, no blue shifts associated with a ring degradation mechanism for RhB were noticed in the spectra [59].

The kinetics for the degradation reaction of RhB over the obtained samples was studied by fitting well with the pseudo-first-order model as follows:
(2)
ln
(
C /
C 0
)
=
k
o b s
t

where C 0 and C are the initial concentration and the remained concentration at time of t, respectively. In addition, kobs (min−1) is the apparent first-order rate constant. Fig. 9a shows the calculated rate constants under visible light. The degradation rate constants of RhB over the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts are 10.8 × 10–4 min–1, 60.1 × 10–4 min–1, 137 × 10–4 min–1, 95.4 × 10–4 min–1, and 471 × 10–4 min–1, respectively. Among the samples, the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, which is about 43.5, 7.8, 4.9, and 3.4 times higher than that of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, and gCN-NS/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts, respectively.

The recombination behaviors of the photoinduced charge carriers in the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) photocatalysts were investigated by PL spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 9b, all the samples exhibited a broad emission peak in the range of 400–550 nm. However, after depositing the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles over the gCN, intensity of the PL emission was decreased, suggesting that the recombination of charge carriers in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite was largely suppressed compared with other samples. Thus, it can be concluded that the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles play an important role in hindering the charge carriers from fast recombination over the gCN.

The textural properties of the gCN, gCN-NS, gCN-NS/CDs, gCN-NS/BiOI (30%), and gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) samples were obtained from nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms, as shown in Fig. 9c and are presented in Table 2. It can be seen that the gCN-NS possesses a high specific surface area (S BET = 88.08 m2/g) compared with the gCN (S BET = 14.56 m2/g), reflecting a high degree of exfoliation. However, after depositing the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles on the surface of gCN-NS, a remarkable decrease in the specific surface area was observed due to the blocking of some pores of the gCN-NS sample by the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles. Although the specific surface area of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite is lower than that of the gCN-NS, it exhibited much higher surface area when compared with the gCN photocatalyst. Hence, it can be concluded that the porous properties are one of the main parameters affecting the photocatalytic activity. As confirmed by the UV–vis and PL results, the enhanced absorption of visible light and effective suppression of photoinduced charge carriers from the recombination along with the improved specific surface area, relative to the gCN, played an important role in enhancing the photocatalytic activity.

Based on the obtained results, a plausible mechanism for the substantially improved photocatalytic activity of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite is proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 10, through absorption of visible light, gCN and BiOI are excited to generate e‒/h+ pairs due to their suitable band gap energies of 2.7 and 1.74 eV, respectively. Since the CB of gCN-NS is more negative than the CB of BiOI, electrons move from gCN-NS to BiOI, leading to the accumulation of negative charges over BiOI and holes over gCN-NS. Because of their high conductivity, CDs act as a mediator for the transfer of electrons from gCN to BiOI [60]. A few number of electrons in the CB of BiOI are consumed to form •OH radicals through the reaction with adsorbed molecules of oxygen (E °(O2/H2O2) = + 0.695 eV vs. NHE) [61]. Simultaneously, a few number of electrons in the CB of gCN-NS may also generate •O2 − species (E °(O2/ • O2
− ) = − 0.33 eV vs. NHE) [62]. Notably, the photoinduced holes move to the surface of gCN-NS to directly oxidize the adsorbed RhB molecules to produce various degradation products. The efficient transfer of photoinduced electrons and holes in the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite leads to the separation of these charges, prolonging the lifetime of charge carriers and enhancing the photocatalytic performance.

In order to detect the main active species in the photocatalytic reaction, trapping experiments were performed to trap the photogenerated holes, superoxide anion radicals, and hydroxyl radicals through the addition of 1.0 mM of ammonium oxalate (AO), 2-propanol (2-PrOH), and benzoquinone (BQ), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 11, the photodegradation rate constants in the presence of BQ, AO, and 2-PrOH were diminished from 471 × 10–4 min–1 to 10.2 × 10–4, 15.3 × 10–4, and 163 × 10–4 min–1, respectively. Due to the substantial influence of BQ and AO on the photocatalytic performance of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite, it can be concluded that in the photodegradation reaction of RhB, superoxide anion radicals and holes were actively involved compared with hydroxyl radicals.

To study the effect of calcination temperature on the photocatalytic performance, the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite was calcined at different temperatures, and the photocatalytic activity was evaluated over the ternary photocatalyst (Fig. 12a). Obviously, the photocatalyst without any calcination exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, and the photodegradation rate constant of RhB was continuously decreased from 471 × 10–4 min–1 to 34.7 × 10–4 min–1 after calcination at 400 °C. To disclose the effect of calcination temperature, the SEM images and PL spectra of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite before and after calcination at 400 °C are shown in Fig. 12b–d. As can be seen, after calcination, the deposited CDs and BiOI nanoparticles aggregated to form larger particles. Fig. 12d shows that after calcination process, the intensity of the PL spectrum sharply increased. Hence, it can be concluded that after the calcination process, the formed heterojunctions between counterparts of the nanocomposite were destructed, which had the major role in efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance of the calcined nanocomposite was substantially diminished.

In order to determine the stability of the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite, the consecutive photocatalytic experiments for the degradation of RhB were carried out for five cycles, and the results were displayed in Fig. 13. It was observed that the removal efficiency of RhB after fifth cycle did not show a considerable decrease, indicating that the ternary photocatalyst had remarkable stability during the photodegradation reactions.

To study ability of the ternary gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite for the removal of other organic pollutants, parallel experiments were conducted with MB, MO, and fuchsine under visible-light illumination. As shown in Fig. 14, among all the samples, the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite has the highest photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of these organic pollutants. The photodegradation rate constants of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine over the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI (30%) nanocomposite are about 29.5, 17.7, 10.9, and 16.5 times higher than that of the gCN, respectively. Hence, as a promising visible-light-active photocatalyst, the prepared ternary nanocomposite has an excellent potential to be applied in the removal of organic pollutants from contaminated water.

In summary, the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposites were successfully synthesized by depositing the CDs and BiOI nanoparticles over gCN nanosheets. The fabricated nanocomposites exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance towards the photodegradation of different organic pollutants under visible-light illumination. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the ternary nanocomposite prepared with 30 wt% of BiOI in photodegradation of RhB, MB, MO, and fuchsine was about 29.5, 17.7, 10.9, and 16.5-folds greater than that of the gCN-NS, whereas it was nearly 5.0, 4.28, 4.32, and 7.1-fold greater than that of the gCN nanosheets, respectively. Based on the obtained results, it was found that the gCN-NS/CDs/BiOI nanocomposite has a substantial ability to absorb visible light and to suppress the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers, resulting in the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support from the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili. In addition, the helpful discussions of Dr. Mirabbos Hojamberdiev are highly acknowledged.
